content.text: Key rotation is the practice of replacing a Keystore encryption key — and re-encrypting all secrets that were protected by it — on a regular schedule or in response to a security event. Secure provisioning is the complementary operation: getting a session token from the backend onto the device in the first place, encrypted immediately under a fresh Keystore key. Together they ensure that the cryptographic material protecting access to your Claude backend proxy has a bounded lifetime and is replaced cleanly when circumstances change.

These patterns protect the session token stored on the device — the credential that authenticates to your backend proxy, which in turn holds the Claude API key. The Claude API key never touches the device; rotating Keystore keys cannot protect a secret that should not be on-device.

attrs.alt: Key rotation state machine: triggers (KeyPermanentlyInvalidated, scheduled, server signal, re-login) driving 6-step rotation protocol with error rollback path and first-run provisioning callout
attrs.title: Key Rotation State Machine and Secure Provisioning

content.text: Provisioning is the one-time operation that brings a session token onto the device after successful authentication. The key must be generated before the token is received, so it is never stored in plaintext even transiently — the only plain form of the token exists in the process memory between receipt and encryption.
Kotlin strings are immutable and interned by the JVM, so you cannot zero them from memory. For higher-assurance provisioning, receive the token as a
before the function returns. This is strictly stronger for long-lived secrets, though the JVM garbage collector may copy heap objects regardless.

Rotation must be atomic from the app's perspective: at any point after a crash, either the old key is still active or the new key is. It must never leave both keys active simultaneously or neither key active. Achieve this with a version marker written only after the new ciphertext is safely persisted.
) rather than incrementing version numbers. The committed version is always the one stored in a prefs key (
still points to the old key, a previous rotation crashed mid-commit — recover by re-running from step 5.
Scheduled rotation runs even when the app is in the background.

is the right tool: it survives process death, respects battery constraints, and is guaranteed to run eventually. Rotation requires biometric auth, which needs a visible Activity, so the worker signals the need for rotation rather than performing it directly — the app then shows the biometric prompt on next foreground.
and show a rationale dialog before triggering the biometric flow that drives
. After a successful rotation, clear the flag and write

Your backend can signal that a client's session token must be rotated — for example, after detecting anomalous Claude API usage or after a server-side credential breach. Encode this signal as a structured error in your API response so the client can detect and respond to it.
When biometric enrollment changes (new fingerprint added, all fingerprints removed), keys created with

(the default) become permanently invalid. You cannot recover the encrypted session token — the decryption key is gone. The recovery path is: clear all stored ciphertext, delete Keystore entries, and force a fresh login.
Deleting the old key before writing the new ciphertext.

This is the most destructive bug in rotation code. If the app crashes after deleting K1 but before persisting C2, the session token is unrecoverable. Always write C2 + IV2 to durable storage before deleting K1.
Using a single Keystore alias for both old and new keys during rotation.
with an existing alias silently overwrites the key in the Keystore, but any existing ciphertext encrypted under the old key is now unrecoverable. Use a second alias (
) for the new key during the rotation window.
Running rotation on the main thread.

Key generation, Cipher operations, and network calls to the backend are all blocking. A rotation that takes 200–800 ms will cause an ANR if triggered on the main thread. All rotation logic must run on
; only the biometric UI interaction needs the main thread.
Not handling partial rotation on app restart.
If the process dies between writing C2 and committing the active alias, the app restarts with
set but the old alias still active. Check for this in
and resume rotation at step 5 (commit the pending token).

Triggering a rotation inside a network call or compose recomposition creates unexpected latency and race conditions. Treat rotation as a dedicated, sequenced operation driven by an explicit user action (resuming the app after the WorkManager flag is set), not an inline side-effect.

Secure provisioning generates the Keystore key before receiving the session token from the backend. The token exists in plaintext only transiently in process memory; the ciphertext is persisted immediately.
Key rotation is a 6-step protocol: biometric → generate K2 → get T2 from backend → encrypt → write ciphertext atomically → delete K1. If any step fails, delete K2 and leave K1 + C1 intact.
pref as the commit point. Generating a new key under the same alias destroys the old ciphertext irrecoverably.
Use WorkManager for scheduled rotation (e.g. every 30 days). The worker sets a flag; the biometric UI runs on next foreground. Rotation itself must run on
fires (biometric enrollment changed), the decryption key is gone. Clear all ciphertext, delete all Keystore entries, and send the user through a fresh login to re-provision a new session token.
